concepts.name: Classification of Hydrocarbons
concepts.explanation: Hydrocarbons are compounds containing only carbon and hydrogen. They are classified into: (i) Saturated (alkanes, cycloalkanes) - single bonds only; (ii) Unsaturated (alkenes, alkynes) - contain double or triple bonds; and (iii) Aromatic (arenes) - contain benzene ring or related structures.

concepts.name: Alkanes – General Formula and Structure
concepts.explanation: Alkanes are saturated open-chain hydrocarbons with the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. Methane (CH₄) is the first member. Carbon is sp³ hybridised with tetrahedral geometry (bond angle 109.5°). C-C bond length is 154 pm, C-H bond length is 112 pm.

concepts.name: Nomenclature of Alkanes
concepts.explanation: Straight-chain alkanes are named with prefixes (meth-, eth-, prop-, but-, pent-, etc.) and suffix '-ane'. Branched alkanes: identify the longest chain, number to give lowest locants to substituents, name substituents alphabetically.

concepts.name: Isomerism in Alkanes – Chain Isomerism
concepts.explanation: Alkanes with four or more carbon atoms show chain isomerism. C₄H₁₀ has two isomers: n-butane (continuous chain) and isobutane (branched). C₅H₁₂ has three isomers: n-pentane, isopentane, and neopentane. Number of isomers increases with carbon count.

concepts.name: Primary, Secondary, Tertiary and Quaternary Carbon Atoms
concepts.explanation: A carbon atom is classified based on the number of carbon atoms attached to it. Primary (1°) – attached to one carbon; Secondary (2°) – attached to two carbons; Tertiary (3°) – attached to three carbons; Quaternary (4°) – attached to four carbons.

concepts.name: Preparation of Alkanes – Hydrogenation of Unsaturated Hydrocarbons
concepts.explanation: Alkenes and alkynes add hydrogen in the presence of catalysts (Pt, Pd, or Ni) to form alkanes. This is called hydrogenation. Example: CH₂=CH₂ + H₂ → CH₃–CH₃ (ethene to ethane).

concepts.name: Preparation of Alkanes – From Alkyl Halides (Reduction)
concepts.explanation: Alkyl halides (except fluorides) are reduced by Zn and dilute HCl to alkanes. Example: CH₃Cl + H₂ → CH₄ + HCl. This is a substitution reaction where halogen is replaced by hydrogen.

concepts.name: Preparation of Alkanes – Wurtz Reaction
concepts.explanation: Alkyl halides react with sodium metal in dry ether to form higher alkanes with an even number of carbon atoms. Example: 2CH₃Br + 2Na → CH₃–CH₃ + 2NaBr. This reaction is not suitable for preparing alkanes with odd number of carbon atoms.

concepts.name: Preparation of Alkanes – Decarboxylation
concepts.explanation: Sodium salts of carboxylic acids on heating with soda lime (NaOH + CaO) give alkanes with one carbon atom less than the parent acid. This is called decarboxylation. Example: CH₃COONa + NaOH → CH₄ + Na₂CO₃.

concepts.name: Preparation of Alkanes – Kolbe's Electrolytic Method
concepts.explanation: Electrolysis of aqueous sodium or potassium salt of a carboxylic acid gives an alkane at the anode with an even number of carbon atoms. Example: 2CH₃COONa + 2H₂O → CH₃–CH₃ + 2CO₂ + H₂ + 2NaOH.

concepts.name: Physical Properties of Alkanes
concepts.explanation: Alkanes are non-polar, colourless, odourless, and insoluble in water (like dissolves like). C₁–C₄ are gases, C₅–C₁₇ are liquids, C₁₈ and above are solids at 298 K. Boiling point increases with molecular mass and decreases with branching.

concepts.name: Chemical Properties – Halogenation of Alkanes (Free Radical Mechanism)
concepts.explanation: Alkanes react with halogens (Cl₂, Br₂) in the presence of light or heat to form haloalkanes. The reaction proceeds via a free radical chain mechanism: (1) Initiation – Cl₂ → 2Cl•; (2) Propagation – Cl• + CH₄ → CH₃• + HCl, CH₃• + Cl₂ → CH₃Cl + Cl•; (3) Termination – radicals combine.
concepts.formula_reaction: CH₄ + Cl₂ → CH₃Cl + HCl; CH₃Cl + Cl₂ → CH₂Cl₂ + HCl, etc.

concepts.name: Chemical Properties – Combustion of Alkanes
concepts.explanation: Alkanes burn in excess oxygen to give CO₂ and H₂O with the release of large amounts of heat (exothermic). They are used as fuels. Incomplete combustion gives carbon black. General equation: CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ + (3n+1)/2 O₂ → nCO₂ + (n+1)H₂O.

concepts.name: Chemical Properties – Controlled Oxidation of Alkanes
concepts.explanation: Alkanes can be oxidised under controlled conditions to give useful products: (i) CH₄ + O₂ → 2CH₃OH (Cu/523K/100 atm); (ii) CH₄ + O₂ → HCHO + H₂O (Mo₂O₃); (iii) 2CH₃CH₃ + 3O₂ → 2CH₃COOH + 2H₂O.

concepts.name: Chemical Properties – Isomerisation of Alkanes
concepts.explanation: n-Alkanes on heating with anhydrous AlCl₃ and HCl gas isomerise to branched chain alkanes. Example: n-hexane → 2-methylpentane + 3-methylpentane. This is important for improving the octane number of petrol.

concepts.name: Chemical Properties – Aromatisation (Reforming) of Alkanes
concepts.explanation: n-Alkanes with six or more carbon atoms, on heating to 773K at 10-20 atm pressure in the presence of Cr₂O₃/Mo₂O₃/V₂O₅ supported on alumina, undergo dehydrogenation and cyclisation to give aromatic hydrocarbons (benzene and its homologues).

concepts.name: Chemical Properties – Pyrolysis (Cracking) of Alkanes
concepts.explanation: Higher alkanes on heating to high temperatures decompose into lower alkanes and alkenes. Example: C₆H₁₄ → C₆H₁₂ + H₂ or C₃H₆ + C₂H₄ + CH₄. This is a free radical reaction and is used in petroleum refining to produce gasoline.

concepts.name: Conformations of Alkanes – Introduction
concepts.explanation: Conformations are different spatial arrangements of atoms that can be interconverted by rotation around a C-C single bond. Rotation is restricted by torsional strain (1-20 kJ mol⁻¹). Infinite conformations are possible, but two extreme forms are staggered and eclipsed.

concepts.name: Conformations – Sawhorse and Newman Projections
concepts.explanation: Sawhorse projection: the C-C bond is drawn diagonally; front carbon is at the lower end, rear carbon at the upper end. Newman projection: viewed along the C-C bond; front carbon is a point, rear carbon is a circle; bonds are drawn at 120° to each other.

concepts.name: Conformations of Ethane – Staggered vs Eclipsed
concepts.explanation: In staggered conformation, H atoms are as far apart as possible (dihedral angle 60°), giving minimum repulsion and maximum stability. In eclipsed conformation, H atoms are as close together as possible (dihedral angle 0°), giving maximum repulsion and minimum stability. Energy difference ≈ 12.5 kJ mol⁻¹.

concepts.name: Alkenes – General Formula and Structure of Double Bond
concepts.explanation: Alkenes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ and contain a C=C double bond. The double bond consists of one σ bond (sp²-sp² head-on overlap) and one π bond (p-p sideways overlap). Bond length is 134 pm; bond angle is 120°. The π bond is weaker and more reactive.

concepts.name: Nomenclature of Alkenes
concepts.explanation: The longest chain containing the double bond is selected and numbered from the end nearer to the double bond. The suffix '-ene' replaces '-ane'. The position of the double bond is indicated by the number of the first doubly bonded carbon.

concepts.name: Isomerism in Alkenes – Structural Isomerism
concepts.explanation: Alkenes show chain isomerism and position isomerism. C₄H₈ has three structural isomers: but-1-ene, but-2-ene, and 2-methylprop-1-ene. C₅H₁₀ has five structural isomers.

concepts.name: Isomerism in Alkenes – Geometrical (Cis-Trans) Isomerism
concepts.explanation: Geometrical isomerism arises due to restricted rotation around the C=C double bond. When identical groups are on the same side, it is the cis isomer; on opposite sides, it is the trans isomer. Example: cis-but-2-ene (polar, b.p. 277K) and trans-but-2-ene (non-polar, b.p. 274K).

concepts.name: Preparation of Alkenes – From Alkynes (Partial Hydrogenation)
concepts.explanation: Alkynes on partial hydrogenation with Lindlar's catalyst (Pd/BaSO₄ poisoned with quinoline) give cis-alkenes. Reduction with Na/NH₃ (liquid) gives trans-alkenes. Example: HC≡CH + H₂ → H₂C=CH₂.

concepts.name: Preparation of Alkenes – Dehydrohalogenation of Alkyl Halides
concepts.explanation: Alkyl halides on heating with alcoholic KOH eliminate HX to form alkenes. This is a β-elimination reaction. Reactivity order: tertiary > secondary > primary; iodine > bromine > chlorine. Example: CH₃CH₂Cl + alc.KOH → CH₂=CH₂ + KCl + H₂O.

concepts.name: Preparation of Alkenes – Dehalogenation of Vicinal Dihalides
concepts.explanation: Vicinal dihalides (halogens on adjacent carbons) on treatment with zinc metal lose ZnX₂ to form alkenes. Example: CH₂Br–CH₂Br + Zn → CH₂=CH₂ + ZnBr₂. This reaction is known as dehalogenation.

concepts.name: Preparation of Alkenes – Dehydration of Alcohols
concepts.explanation: Alcohols on heating with concentrated H₂SO₄ eliminate H₂O to form alkenes. This is acidic dehydration (β-elimination). Example: CH₃CH₂OH → CH₂=CH₂ + H₂O. Reactivity order: tertiary > secondary > primary.

concepts.name: Physical Properties of Alkenes
concepts.explanation: Alkenes are non-polar, colourless, odourless (except ethene with a faint sweet smell), insoluble in water, soluble in non-polar solvents. C₂–C₄ are gases, C₅–C₁₈ are liquids, higher are solids. Boiling point increases with molecular mass; branching decreases boiling point.

concepts.name: Chemical Properties – Addition of Halogens to Alkenes
concepts.explanation: Halogens (Br₂, Cl₂) add to alkenes to form vicinal dihalides. Bromine in CCl₄ is decolourised (test for unsaturation). Example: CH₂=CH₂ + Br₂ → CH₂Br–CH₂Br. Iodine does not add under normal conditions.

concepts.name: Chemical Properties – Addition of Hydrogen Halides (Markovnikov Rule)
concepts.explanation: HX adds to unsymmetrical alkenes according to Markovnikov rule: the negative part of HX attaches to the carbon with fewer hydrogen atoms. Example: CH₃–CH=CH₂ + HBr → CH₃–CHBr–CH₃ (2-bromopropane, major). The reaction proceeds via the more stable carbocation.

concepts.name: Chemical Properties – Anti-Markovnikov Addition (Peroxide Effect)
concepts.explanation: In the presence of peroxide, HBr adds to unsymmetrical alkenes contrary to Markovnikov rule. Example: CH₃–CH=CH₂ + HBr (peroxide) → CH₃–CH₂–CH₂Br (1-bromopropane). This proceeds via a free radical mechanism. Peroxide effect is not observed with HCl or HI.

concepts.name: Chemical Properties – Addition of Sulphuric Acid
concepts.explanation: Cold concentrated H₂SO₄ adds to alkenes to form alkyl hydrogen sulphates (according to Markovnikov rule). Example: CH₂=CH₂ + H₂SO₄ → C₂H₅HSO₄ (ethyl hydrogen sulphate).

concepts.name: Chemical Properties – Addition of Water (Hydration)
concepts.explanation: Alkenes react with water in the presence of a few drops of concentrated H₂SO₄ to form alcohols (Markovnikov rule). Example: CH₃–C(CH₃)=CH₂ + H₂O → (CH₃)₃COH (2-methylpropan-2-ol).

concepts.name: Chemical Properties – Oxidation of Alkenes
concepts.explanation: Alkenes react with cold dilute KMnO₄ (Baeyer's reagent) to form vicinal glycols (test for unsaturation). With acidic KMnO₄, they are oxidised to ketones and/or acids. Example: CH₂=CH₂ + [O] + H₂O → CH₂OH–CH₂OH.

concepts.name: Chemical Properties – Ozonolysis of Alkenes
concepts.explanation: Ozonolysis of alkenes involves addition of O₃ to form ozonide, followed by cleavage with Zn/H₂O to give carbonyl compounds. This reaction helps locate the position of the double bond. Example: CH₃CH=CH₂ → CH₃CHO + HCHO.

concepts.name: Chemical Properties – Polymerisation of Alkenes
concepts.explanation: Alkenes undergo polymerisation (addition polymerisation) at high temperature, high pressure, and in the presence of a catalyst to form polymers. Example: n(CH₂=CH₂) → -(CH₂–CH₂)-ₙ (polythene); n(CH₃–CH=CH₂) → -(CH₂–CH(CH₃))-ₙ (polypropene).

concepts.name: Alkynes – General Formula and Structure of Triple Bond
concepts.explanation: Alkynes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₋₂ and contain a C≡C triple bond. The triple bond consists of one σ bond (sp-sp head-on overlap) and two π bonds (p-p sideways overlap). Bond length is 120 pm; bond angle is 180°. The molecule is linear.

concepts.name: Nomenclature of Alkynes
concepts.explanation: The longest chain containing the triple bond is selected and numbered from the end nearer to the triple bond. The suffix '-yne' replaces '-ane'. The position is indicated by the number of the first triply bonded carbon. Example: HC≡CH (ethyne), CH₃–C≡CH (propyne).

concepts.name: Isomerism in Alkynes
concepts.explanation: Alkynes show position isomerism and chain isomerism. Butyne has two isomers: but-1-yne and but-2-yne (position isomers). C₅H₈ has three isomers: pent-1-yne, pent-2-yne (position isomers), and 3-methylbut-1-yne (chain isomer).

concepts.name: Preparation of Alkynes – From Calcium Carbide
concepts.explanation: Ethyne is prepared industrially by treating calcium carbide with water. Calcium carbide is obtained by heating quick lime (CaO) with coke. CaC₂ + 2H₂O → HC≡CH + Ca(OH)₂. This is used for oxyacetylene welding.

concepts.name: Preparation of Alkynes – From Vicinal Dihalides
concepts.explanation: Vicinal dihalides on treatment with alcoholic KOH undergo dehydrohalogenation to give alkynyl halides, which on further treatment with sodamide (NaNH₂) give alkynes. Example: CH₂Br–CH₂Br → HC≡CH (two steps).

concepts.name: Physical Properties of Alkynes
concepts.explanation: Alkynes are colourless, odourless (except ethyne with a characteristic odour), weakly polar, lighter than water, insoluble in water, soluble in organic solvents. C₂–C₄ are gases, C₅–C₁₂ are liquids, higher are solids.

concepts.name: Chemical Properties – Acidic Character of Alkynes
concepts.explanation: Hydrogen atoms attached to triply bonded carbon (sp hybridised, 50% s-character) are acidic. They react with Na or NaNH₂ to form metal acetylides. Example: HC≡CH + Na → HC≡CNa + ½H₂. Acidity order: HC≡CH > H₂C=CH₂ > CH₃–CH₃.

concepts.name: Chemical Properties – Addition Reactions of Alkynes
concepts.explanation: Alkynes undergo addition reactions similar to alkenes, but two molecules of the addendum add successively. Examples: (i) Hydrogenation: HC≡CH + 2H₂ → CH₃–CH₃; (ii) Halogenation: HC≡CH + Br₂ → CHBr=CHBr → CHBr₂–CHBr₂; (iii) HX addition: HC≡CH + HBr → CH₂=CHBr → CH₃–CHBr₂.

concepts.name: Chemical Properties – Addition of Water to Alkynes
concepts.explanation: Alkynes add one molecule of water in the presence of HgSO₄ and dilute H₂SO₄ at 333 K to form carbonyl compounds. Ethyne gives acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO); propyne gives acetone (CH₃COCH₃). This follows Markovnikov rule.

concepts.name: Chemical Properties – Polymerisation of Alkynes
concepts.explanation: Ethyne undergoes: (i) Linear polymerisation to form polyacetylene (conducting polymer); (ii) Cyclic polymerisation – three molecules of ethyne pass through a red-hot iron tube at 873K to form benzene. This is a method to synthesise benzene from aliphatic compounds.

concepts.name: Aromatic Hydrocarbons – Nomenclature
concepts.explanation: Monosubstituted benzenes are named as substituent + benzene (e.g., chlorobenzene, nitrobenzene). Disubstituted benzenes use ortho (1,2), meta (1,3), and para (1,4) prefixes. For trisubstituted, use lowest locant rule.

concepts.name: Structure of Benzene – Kekule Structure and Resonance
concepts.explanation: Kekule proposed a cyclic structure with alternate single and double bonds. However, benzene is a resonance hybrid of two Kekule structures. The actual structure is represented by a hexagon with a circle inside, indicating delocalised π electrons.

concepts.name: Aromaticity – Huckel Rule
concepts.explanation: A compound is aromatic if it is: (i) planar; (ii) completely conjugated (all atoms in the ring have p orbitals); and (iii) has (4n+2) π electrons in the ring (Huckel rule). Examples: benzene (6π, n=1), cyclobutadiene (4π, anti-aromatic), cyclooctatetraene (8π, non-aromatic).

concepts.name: Preparation of Benzene
concepts.explanation: Benzene is prepared by: (i) Cyclic polymerisation of ethyne: 3HC≡CH → C₆H₆ (red-hot Fe tube, 873K); (ii) Decarboxylation of sodium benzoate: C₆H₅COONa + NaOH → C₆H₆ + Na₂CO₃.

concepts.name: Physical Properties of Aromatic Hydrocarbons
concepts.explanation: Arenes are non-polar, colourless liquids or solids with a characteristic aroma. They are immiscible with water but miscible with organic solvents. They burn with a sooty flame (due to high carbon content).

concepts.name: Electrophilic Substitution Reactions – Nitration of Benzene
concepts.explanation: Benzene reacts with a nitrating mixture (conc. HNO₃ + conc. H₂SO₄) to give nitrobenzene. The electrophile is the nitronium ion (NO₂⁺), generated by protonation of HNO₃ followed by loss of water.

concepts.name: Electrophilic Substitution Reactions – Halogenation of Benzene
concepts.explanation: Benzene reacts with halogens (Cl₂, Br₂) in the presence of a Lewis acid catalyst (FeCl₃, AlCl₃, FeBr₃) to form haloarenes. Example: C₆H₆ + Cl₂ → C₆H₅Cl + HCl (anhyd. FeCl₃).
concepts.formula_reaction: C₆H₆ + X₂ → C₆H₅X + HX (Lewis acid catalyst)

concepts.name: Electrophilic Substitution Reactions – Sulphonation of Benzene
concepts.explanation: Benzene reacts with fuming sulphuric acid (oleum) to give benzenesulphonic acid. The electrophile is SO₃ (sulphur trioxide). Example: C₆H₆ + SO₃ → C₆H₅SO₃H.

concepts.name: Electrophilic Substitution Reactions – Friedel-Crafts Alkylation
concepts.explanation: Benzene reacts with alkyl halides in the presence of anhydrous AlCl₃ to form alkylbenzenes. The electrophile is the carbocation (R⁺). Example: C₆H₆ + CH₃Cl → C₆H₅CH₃ + HCl. Rearrangement may occur (e.g., n-propyl chloride gives isopropylbenzene).

concepts.name: Electrophilic Substitution Reactions – Friedel-Crafts Acylation
concepts.explanation: Benzene reacts with acyl halides (RCOCl) in the presence of anhydrous AlCl₃ to form acylbenzenes (ketones). The electrophile is the acylium ion (R-C≡O⁺). Example: C₆H₆ + CH₃COCl → C₆H₅COCH₃ + HCl.

concepts.name: Mechanism of Electrophilic Substitution Reactions
concepts.explanation: The mechanism involves three steps: (1) Generation of electrophile (E⁺); (2) Formation of σ-complex (arenium ion) – electrophile attacks the ring, breaking aromaticity; (3) Removal of proton to restore aromaticity. The σ-complex is resonance-stabilised.

concepts.name: Addition Reactions of Benzene
concepts.explanation: Under vigorous conditions, benzene can undergo addition: (i) Hydrogenation: C₆H₆ + 3H₂ → C₆H₁₂ (Ni, high temp/pressure); (ii) Chlorination: C₆H₆ + 3Cl₂ → C₆H₆Cl₆ (benzene hexachloride, UV light).

concepts.name: Directive Influence of Substituents – Ortho/Para Directing Groups
concepts.explanation: Groups that donate electrons via resonance (+R effect) direct the incoming electrophile to ortho and para positions. Examples: -OH, -NH₂, -OCH₃, -CH₃ (alkyl groups). These are activating groups (increase electron density). Halogens are also o/p directing but deactivating due to strong -I effect.

concepts.name: Directive Influence of Substituents – Meta Directing Groups
concepts.explanation: Groups that withdraw electrons via resonance (-R effect) and have strong -I effect direct the incoming electrophile to the meta position. Examples: -NO₂, -CN, -CHO, -COR, -COOH, -SO₃H. These are deactivating groups (decrease electron density).

concepts.name: Carcinogenicity and Toxicity of Hydrocarbons
concepts.explanation: Benzene and polynuclear aromatic hydrocarbons (e.g., benzopyrene) are carcinogenic (cancer-causing). They are formed by incomplete combustion of organic materials like tobacco, coal, and petroleum. They enter the body and damage DNA.
